dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a possible cardiac arrest at a residential home in [CITY].
Hello, I'm Brad, an EMT with Rescue Squad 3. What brought you to call 911 tonight?
My dad was found unresponsive upstairs. We've been doing CPR.
Can you tell me his age and any known medical conditions?
He's 72 years old. Knew him to have Parkinson's, kidney disease, and high blood pressure.
Has he been conscious at all since you found him?
No. He wasn't breathing and didn't respond at all when we found him.
Sir, we're confirming airway placement with ETCO2 monitoring now.
Oropharyngeal Airway placed and secured at 21 cm.
Does anyone notice movement in his chest?
Nope, chest is flat and no rise. No breathing.
Pulse is very fast - 158 per minute - and BP low at 91 over 41.
Pulse has increased slightly - now at 164 per minute.
Respirations still at 0.
Blood pressure rose a little to 106 over 18, but still weak to palpate.
Oxygen saturation is critically low - 54%. We'll recheck later.
EKG shows no heart motion - asystole.
Blood sugar was 112 at first, then dropped to 0 on the next check.
Starting compressions with the AutoPulse machine now. RFD, assist if possible.
Directed RFD to continue compressions with AutoPulse. Good squeeze depth reported.
Preparing for intubation. Video laryngoscope in hand. Using a size 7.5 oral tube.
Visual confirmation of vocal cords with tube placement. Hold that, Brad, securing it now.
Monitored with 4-lead EKG again - rhythm still asystole.
IV in the right arm unsuccessful. External Jugular is the next attempt.
External Jugular IV also unsuccessful. Now preparing for intraosseous access in the left humeral head.
Repeating rhythm checks - still asystole with no spontaneous return of pulse.
Administering first dose of epinephrine 1:10,000 through the IO site - 1 mg.
Second epinephrine dose done at 00:49. Timing checks are within protocol.
Pulse remains fast - 164 per minute - rhythm still flat. SpO2 dropped further to 20%.
We are on the third epinephrine dose. Timing was successful - 3-5 minutes in between.
Third dose given. No improvement noted. Continuing following the protocol as instructed.
Still flatline, thready pulse, and pale, cool skin. Oxygen is only 20%.
Any last known normal time or unusual activity before this event?
Last seen well at 10 PM. No allergies, medications, or unusual behavior noted.
No food or drink reported since yesterday.
Skin is dry and pale. No signs of bruising or rashes.
Pupils are fixed and non-reactive. No signs of airway swelling.
Oral cavity clear, endotracheal tube in place, no obstruction.
No signs of leak, and thorax remains flat.
Abdomen non-tender, but no physical status reported due to time frame and event.
No reported extremity or back injuries from on-scene assessment.
Following Protocol 2-7 - adult cardiac arrest with established IO access.
No indication of pain, as expected in cardiac arrest per protocol.
Still no additional input since 10 PM timeframe.
Wait for physician confirmation about pronouncement. Transport not considered at this time.
Securing for on-scene pronouncement. No movement for transport.
Reached out to Dr. Taylor for pronouncement status now.
Dr. Taylor is evaluating the response and the patient status now.
Dr. Taylor has confirmed cease of resuscitation. All steps completed per field protocol.
All fields steps and notes finalized. Patient is deceased on scene per legal report.
No signs of prolonged airway obstruction or trauma.
Airway secured with a commercial device. Confirming placement now.
Repeat rhythm check, EKG still asystolic. No improvement observed.
IO line successfully placed in left humeral head. Proceeding with epinephrine administration.
SpO2 at 20. No improvement despite ongoing efforts.
No neck trauma or swelling detected.
Blood pressure now at 106 over 18 mmHg - no real functional perfusion.
Pulse again at 164. Weak and thready.
Third epinephrine dose administered at 00:51. Still no positive response.
Blood sugar at trace - essentially 0 on latest result.
Rechecking all vitals - rhythm, pulse, and saturation remain unchanged.
Breath sounds are clear on both sides. ETT is in good location.
No bite marks, rashes or purpura. Skin remains pale, dry, and cool.
Protocol remains 2-7 until legal pronouncement is received. Staying with the standard of care.
Waiting for Dr. Taylor to confirm death on-scene. Device setup for documentation.
Preparing documentation for Dr. Taylor's confirmation. No further resuscitation planned.
Final notes to be sent for physician review and pronouncement signing.
No signs of rigor mortis or pooling yet. Patient was newly found apneic.
Unresponsive. No eye-opening, motor, or verbal activity. Glasgow 3.
No immediate airway threats found. OPA secured in place.
Cardiac rhythm remains globally asystolic. Flatline per repeating 4-lead.
Negative for neck stiffness or deformities. No signs of trauma.
SpO2 recheck at 20%. Still consistent with terminal status.
No signs of jaundice or tearing. Skin turgor is dry, no edema.
No signs of open wounds, angulation, or peripheral injury.
IO access is the only viable route for drug administration given multiple IV attempts.
IO secured well. Checking for any mechanical looseness or flow issues.
No palpable response after epinephrine. Pulse remains thready.
All sounds appear normal. No signs of breath leak or movement.
No foreign bodies, discharge, or.sulcus suctioning. Oral airway in good place.
No new information; last known functional time remains at 10 PM.
Full abdominal assessment not feasible within the time frame due to the standing status.
Epinephrine given per protocol and timing at all intervals as documented.
No new developments in drug or clinical responses observed after the last dose.
No transport planned based on paramedic and medical control direction. Remaining on scene.
No movement needed. Secondary transfer handled per local protocol.
Law enforcement secured on scene for oversight. All steps followed with guidance.
Death certificate form signed locally. No conflict on scene.
Documentation is complete for EMT and physician sign-off.
Equipment is ready for turn-in. Cleaned all non-contaminated tools.
No signs of prolonged accumulation or pooling.
Cardiac waveform remains flatline after confirmation by 4-lead EKG.
No thorax trauma signs. Good breath - symmetric and shallow.
No sustained pulse - all readings indicated accelerated and weak response.
Skin stays pale. No signs of changes following interventions.
Glucose at zero; confirmed hypoglycemic on second check.
Rechecked ET tube, still placed, with good waveform.
No pops, clunks or manipulative movement signs expected in vital structures.
Protocol decision remains unified. No deflection from adult cardiac arrest steps.
No subjective or objective signs of pain. No pain response expected.
EMT calls physician to confirm legal cessation per on-scene data.
Physician confirms legal status by 00:55. Termination at scene with no further resuscitation.
No movement needed. Secondary transfer handled as per REMSA.
Remind family for acknowledgments. Legal team has closed this incident.
Nothing more to be done. Paper and device reports ready for end of shift report.
No palpation attempted or documented. No data to act on due to critical status.
Cutdown not possible per skill limitation and protocol.
Airway remains protected with OPA and ETT.
Still no rise or fall of the torso. No spontaneous motions.
Circulation follows guidelines. No real pulse pressure or meaningful signs.
Absolutely no motor activity. GCS unchanged.
No further actions planned or recommended per physician guidance.
Carried out interventions as per protocol. All meds and timing correct per field log.
Oxygen saturation persistently low. SpO2 20%, no response to administration.
No hospital transport necessary. No value in movement per medical direction.
Vehicle secured for scene closure as standard procedure.
Full report submitted. Legal official closing the case.
No unexpected trauma signs. Death via cardiac arrest.
No change in vitals or health status. Steady decline as expected.
Documented in shift log for closing the case per EMT field directive.
